skill.name: email-composer
skill.description: Draft polished professional emails for requests, follow-ups, technical updates, customer support, meetings, apologies, and announcements.
skill.summary: Compose clear business emails with subject lines, tone guidance, and structured templates.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged Markdown code fences as Ruby backtick execution and ordinary prose as weak cryptography and reconnaissance indicators. Review found only email examples, tone guidance, etiquette advice, and composition checklists, with no executable code, network access, filesystem access, secret handling, or prompt injection attempt.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Fences Flagged as Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged locations are Markdown fenced email examples, not Ruby shell backtick execution. They contain subjects, greetings, body text, and sign-offs for professional email templates.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact locations are Markdown code fences and email template text. No shell command, interpreter invocation, variable interpolation, or execution instruction is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Pattern
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged lines are the frontmatter description and a bullet asking for desired email tone. They do not mention hashing, encryption, MD5, SHA-1, DES, or any cryptographic operation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Semantic review of both lines confirms they are plain writing instructions. There is no cryptographic context or code path to evaluate.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Pattern
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged line advises writers to avoid contractions in formal email. It does not request system information, execute commands, inspect the host, or enumerate files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is part of tone guidance for formal writing. No reconnaissance behavior or instruction appears in the surrounding section.

content.value_statement: Writing clear business emails takes time and can create tone problems. This skill provides structured templates, subject line guidance, and etiquette checks for common workplace messages.
actual_capabilities: Drafts professional emails from purpose, recipient relationship, key points, and desired tone.
Provides templates for requests, follow-ups, technical updates, support replies, and meeting requests.
Suggests formal, casual, urgent, and professional tone choices.
Improves subject lines with clear and specific wording.
Applies email etiquette checks for recipients, attachments, calls to action, and proofreading.
Offers scenario templates for polite declines, apologies, and positive announcements.
limitations: Does not send emails or connect to inboxes.
Does not verify facts, dates, order details, or technical claims.
Requires the user to provide recipient context and key message points.
Does not replace legal, HR, or compliance review for sensitive messages.

use_cases.title: Write customer support replies
use_cases.description: Create clear responses that acknowledge the issue, explain the resolution, and set expectations.

use_cases.title: Send project updates
use_cases.description: Turn status notes into concise updates with timelines, blockers, next steps, and calls to action.

use_cases.title: Prepare professional follow-ups
use_cases.description: Draft polite follow-up emails after proposals, meetings, unanswered requests, or pending approvals.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a simple email
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft a professional email asking [recipient] for [request]. Use a [formal/casual/friendly] tone and include these points: [points].

prompt_templates.title: Improve my draft
prompt_templates.prompt: Rewrite this email to be clearer, shorter, and more professional. Keep the meaning and make the call to action obvious: [draft].

prompt_templates.title: Create a situation-specific message
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a [follow-up/support reply/technical update/meeting request] email for [audience]. Include subject line options, key context, next steps, and a suitable sign-off.

prompt_templates.title: Adapt tone for multiple recipients
prompt_templates.prompt: Create three versions of this email for an executive, a customer, and an internal teammate. Adjust tone, detail level, and call to action for each audience: [context].

output_examples.input: Ask a vendor for an updated implementation timeline after a missed milestone.
output_examples.output:
Subject: Updated timeline requested for implementation milestone

Hi [Name],

I am following up on the implementation milestone planned for [date]. Could you please share the current status, the revised completion date, and any blockers that may affect delivery?

This will help our team adjust internal planning and communicate expectations clearly.

Best regards,
[Your name]

output_examples.input: Write a short customer support apology for a delayed order.
output_examples.output:
Subject: Update on your delayed order

Hi [Customer name],

Thank you for your patience. I am sorry your order has taken longer than expected.

We have reviewed the shipment and expect delivery by [date]. We will send another update if anything changes.

Best regards,
[Your name]

output_examples.input: Create a technical maintenance notice for internal teams.
output_examples.output:
Subject: Scheduled API maintenance on [date]

Team,

We will perform scheduled API maintenance on [date] from [time] to [time] [timezone]. During this window, API endpoints may be unavailable.

Please notify affected users, confirm retry logic is working, and monitor services after maintenance ends.

Thanks,
[Your name]

best_practices: Provide the recipient relationship, desired tone, key points, and deadline before asking for a draft.
Review every draft for factual accuracy, sensitive details, and organization-specific language.
Ask for subject line options when the email needs action, urgency, or executive visibility.
anti_patterns: Do not ask for a final email without giving the audience, purpose, and required outcome.
Do not send generated drafts without checking names, dates, links, and attachments.
Do not use urgent wording unless the recipient truly needs immediate action.

faq.question: Can this skill send emails for me?
faq.answer: No. It drafts and improves email text only. You must send the message through your own email client.

faq.question: What information should I provide?
faq.answer: Provide the purpose, recipient relationship, key points, desired tone, deadline, and any required call to action.

faq.question: Can it write customer support emails?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes support reply patterns with acknowledgement, explanation, resolution steps, and expected outcomes.

faq.question: Can it make an email more formal?
faq.answer: Yes. Ask it to revise your draft for formal tone, complete sentences, professional language, and suitable greetings.

faq.question: Does it verify facts in my message?
faq.answer: No. You must confirm facts, dates, numbers, order details, links, and policy language before sending.

faq.question: Is it useful with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill provides reusable writing guidance that can be used in Claude, Codex, and Claude Code sessions.
